detailed_units.title: Greetings, Time, and Pronouns
detailed_units.can_do: I can greet people at any time of day and use basic pronouns.

new_grammar.point: Personal name marker 'hi' (before a person's name)
new_grammar.explain_en: 'Hi' is a common marker used before a person's name in Waray, often indicating the subject of a sentence or simply introducing the name.
'Kita' means 'we' and includes the person you are speaking to (you and I). 'Kami' means 'we' but excludes the person you are speaking to (I and others, but not you).

phrases.en: We'll be good later (you and I)!
How are they? We're good (not you).
We are good now (you and I).

sentences.war: Maupay nga aga. Ako hi Maria.

sentences.en: I am good. We are good now!

questions.q: What time of day is it?

detailed_units.title: Family, Respect, and Pointers
detailed_units.can_do: I can address extended family and pinpoint locations.

new_grammar.explain_en: These adverbs indicate location relative to the speaker and listener. 'Aadi' means 'here, by me'. 'Aada' means 'there, by you'. 'Didto' means 'over there, far from both of us'.
'Diri' is the general negator in Waray-Waray, meaning 'no' or 'not'. It comes before the word or phrase it negates.

new_vocab.gloss: older brother — also any slightly older male
older sister — also any slightly older female

phrases.en: He/she is not here, he/she goes there.
My grandchild is there, come here!

sentences.en: My mother is there. Come here!

sentences.war: Didto hi tatay ko ngan hi apo ko.
sentences.en: My father and my grandchild are over there.

questions.q: Who is over there with the father?

detailed_units.title: House and Daily Actions
detailed_units.can_do: I can identify house parts and express basic daily actions.

new_grammar.explain_en: The marker 'in' is used before general common nouns, similar to 'the' or 'a/an' in English. In Samar Waray, 'it' is often used instead of 'in'.

new_grammar.point: Possession: akon (my), imo (your sg), iyo (your pl)
new_grammar.explain_en: To show possession, use 'akon' (my/mine), 'imo' (your/yours, singular), and 'iyo' (your/yours, plural). These come before the noun they possess.

new_vocab.note: Wash area common in Samar homes.

sentences.war: Maupay nga aga! Ini in akon balay.
sentences.en: Good day! This is my house.

sentences.war: Sulod ha purtahan, tapos lingkod ha pantaw.
sentences.en: Enter the door, then sit on the back porch.

sentences.war: Adto in iyo kwarto, ngan didto in akon kusina.
sentences.en: That is your (plural) room, and there is my kitchen.

sentences.war: Gawas ha natad, in kudal didto.
sentences.en: Go out to the yard, there is a fence there.

detailed_units.title: Food, Drinks, and Requests
detailed_units.can_do: I can ask for specific foods and make polite requests.

new_grammar.explain_en: To express 'I want' or 'I like', use 'Gusto ko...'. 'Gusto' means 'want' or 'like', and 'ko' is the enclitic pronoun for 'I' (as agent/possessor). For 'you want', it's 'Gusto mo...'. The object usually follows.

The particle 'hin' (or 'sin' in Samar) marks an indefinite, non-specific direct object. It often translates to 'some' or 'a/an' in English, or is simply implied. It's used when you don't mean a specific item, but rather 'some of it' or 'any of it'.

new_vocab.note: Agent/possessor of hiya — confirm Daram form.

phrases.war: Gusto ko hin init nga kape.

phrases.en: Thank you, I want some rice.

phrases.war: Kinahanglan mo ba matugnaw nga tubig?
Kaon hin prutas ngan inom hin tubig.

sentences.war: Hi Lolo Ben gusto hin kape.
Kinahanglan niya hin kan-on ngan isda.
sentences.en: He needs some rice and fish.

sentences.war: Alayon, gusto niya matugnaw nga tubig.

sentences.en: Thank you, Grandpa Ben is happy.

questions.q: Ano an gusto ni Lolo Ben inumon?

detailed_units.title: Numbers and Money
detailed_units.can_do: I can count up to ten and handle basic transactions.

new_grammar.explain_en: To ask for the price of an item, use 'Tagpira ini?' (How much is this?). For plural items, you can use 'ini' or 'ira' (these/those).
The verb 'mapalit' means 'will buy'. It's commonly used to express an intention or future action of buying.

lessons.title: Asking Price and Money
lessons.grammar_focus: Asking price with 'Tagpira ini?'; Future buying with 'mapalit'

phrases.en: How much money do you have?

phrases.war: Mahal an isda, barato an tinapay.
phrases.en: The fish is expensive, the bread is cheap.

sentences.war: Mahal an isda. Barato an tinapay.
sentences.en: The fish is expensive. The bread is cheap.
